description:
# AWSSupport-CollectEKSLinuxNodeStatistics

The **AWSSupport-CollectEKSLinuxNodeStatistics** runbook collects Linux statistics from an EC2 instance that is part of an Amazon EKS cluster, and from a container running on the instance if a **containerd** container ID is specified. The EC2 instance has to be [managed by AWS Systems Manager](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/systems-manager-setting-up-ec2.html).

The host-level Linux statistics collected include:

* OS information.
* Network interface statistics - from `ethtool` and `/sys/class/net/interface/statistics` directory.
* File descriptors counts.
* Ephemeral ports counts.
* A dump of `iptables` rules.
* Check for a full conntrack table.

The container-level Linux statistics include:
* Identifier information - image URI and labels.
* Network interface statistics - from `ethtool` and `/sys/class/net/interface/statistics` directory.
* Traceroute and DNS results if the **NetworkTargets** parameter is populated.
* Packet capture analysis counts - TCP Retransmissions, Out of Order packets etc.

The runbook collects data from various Linux distributions including Amazon Linux 2, Amazon Linux 2023 and Debian/Ubuntu. It uses the latest versions of the following images from the Amazon ECR public gallery:

* [amazon-ecs-network-sidecar](https://gallery.ecr.aws/aws-se/amazon-ecs-network-sidecar) image to gain access to troubleshooting tools.
* [aws-cli](https://gallery.ecr.aws/aws-cli/aws-cli) image to upload the statistics report JSON file and packet capture files to the specified S3 bucket.

**Please note the following:**

* This runbook does not support Fargate instances.
* This runbook may fail if the instance is shutdown or disconnected during execution.

AutomationAssumeRole.description: (Optional) The Amazon Resource Name (ARN) of the AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) role that allows Systems Manager Automation to perform the actions on your behalf. If no role is specified, Systems Manager Automation uses the permissions of the user that starts this runbook.

InstanceId.description: (Required) The ID of the EC2 Instance to collect statistics.

S3BucketName.description: (Required) Name of the S3 bucket to export the JSON output from the EC2 instance as a file.

S3KeyPrefix.description: (Optional) The S3 key prefix (sub-folder) to export the JSON output from the EC2 instance as a file.

S3BucketOwnerRoleArn.description: (Optional) The ARN of the IAM role with permissions to get the Amazon S3 bucket and account block public access settings, bucket encryption configuration, the bucket ACLs, the bucket policy status, and upload objects to the bucket. If this parameter is not specified, the runbook uses the `AutomationAssumeRole` (if specified) or user that starts this runbook (if `AutomationAssumeRole` is not specified). Please see the required permissions section in the runbook description.

S3BucketOwnerAccount.description: (Optional) The AWS account that owns the Amazon S3 bucket. If you do not specify this parameter, the runbook assumes that the bucket is in this account.

ContainerId.description: (Optional) The ID of a container running on the specified EC2 instance.

NetworkTargets.description: (Optional) A comma-separated list of IPv4 addresses and/or DNS names to test DNS resolution, and connectivity using traceroute.

mainSteps.name: CheckBucketAccess
mainSteps.description: Checks if the target Amazon S3 bucket potentially grants **read** and/or **write** `public` access to its objects.

mainSteps.name: AssertInstanceIsSSMManaged
mainSteps.description: Ensures the target EC2 instance is managed by AWS Systems Manager, otherwise the automation ends.

mainSteps.name: VerifyInstanceState
mainSteps.description: Verifies that the EC2 instance is in a running state before attempting to collect statistics.

mainSteps.name: BranchOnVerifyLinuxInstance
mainSteps.description: Verifies that the instance is a Linux instance before proceeding.

mainSteps.name: BranchOnVerifyInstanceRunning
mainSteps.description: Verifies that the instance is in a running state before proceeding.

mainSteps.name: CollectEKSLinuxNodeStatistics
mainSteps.description:
Collects comprehensive Linux statistics from the EC2 instance. The script will:
1. Detect the OS type (Amazon Linux 2, Amazon Linux 2023, Bottlerocket OS, Debian/Ubuntu)
2. Check if the instance is managed by an orchestration system (EKS or ECS)
3. Install necessary tools (jq, traceroute, tcpdump) if not available
4. For orchestrated instances, pull the amazon-ecs-network-sidecar diagnostic image
5. Collect OS information (architecture, name, version)
6. Collect file descriptor statistics
7. Collect ephemeral port information
8. Collect firewall rules (iptables, UFW)
9. Collect network interface statistics
10. Run traceroute tests if targets are specified
11. Perform packet capture if interfaces are specified
12. Collect cgroups statistics
13. Collect conntrack table statistics
14. Compile all data into a JSON structure
15. Clean up any installed packages if on a standalone instance

commands: # Copyright Amazon.com, Inc. or its affiliates. All Rights Reserved.
# Licensed under the Amazon Software License  http://aws.amazon.com/asl/
# Linux Statistics Collection Script for AWS SSM Automation.
# This script collects comprehensive system statistics from a Linux EC2 instances and
# from a container if the CONTAINER_ID variable is populated.
# Diagnostic container properties for troubleshooting Kubernetes nodes and containers.
# Parameters for uploading network packet capture files to Amazon S3.
# Array to track images that already exist on the node.
log "ERROR: Command failed with exit code $exit_code"
# Output a minimal JSON with error information
echo "{\"Output\": \"{\\\"error\\\": \\\"Script failed at line $1 with exit code $exit_code\\\"}\"}"
# Export host OS information to JSON.
# Function to check if instance is managed by an orchestration system.
log "Checking if instance is orchestrated by Amazon EKS..."
log "ERROR: Did not detect EKS orchestration on this instance. Aborting..."
############# Container handling functions for Amazon EKS #############
# Checks with containerd if a container exists.
# - Stops a containerd task (running instance) of the diagnostic container.
# - Removes the diagnostic container from containerd.
# - Removes any leftover snapshots that might prevent container creation.
log "Container found: Image $image, Created at $created_at. Attempting to remove it..."
# Try to kill the task if it's running, but don't fail if there's no task
log "WARN: No running task of the container was found or task kill failed."
# Always try to remove the container, regardless of task kill result
if ctr -n k8s.io containers rm "$container_name" 2>/dev/null; then
log "WARN: Failed to remove container or container was already removed."
# Clean up any leftover snapshots that might prevent container creation
if ctr -n k8s.io snapshots ls | grep -q "$container_name"; then
if ctr -n k8s.io snapshots rm "$container_name" 2>/dev/null; then
log "WARN: Failed to remove snapshot or snapshot was already removed."
# This function pulls the an image into containerd.
if ! ctr -n k8s.io images ls | grep "$image_uri"; then
# This function removes an image from containerd.
log "Skipping removal of $image_uri as it already existed before script's execution."
# This function runs the diagnostic container in containerd.
# --host-network - Container will be created in host's network mode. Cannot be used with the parameters below.
# --container_id <id> - ID of the container to create the diagnostic container with (in the specified namespace).
# Clean up any existing diagnostic container before creating a new one
log "Starting the diagnostic container in host network mode..."
log "Successfully started the diagnostic container in host network mode..."
log "ERROR: Target container ID was not specified."
log "Starting the diagnostic container in target container's network namespace..."
# Get the target container's network namespace and resolv.conf host paths.
# Run the diagnostic container using the above target container parameters.
log "Succesfully started the diagnostic container in the network namespace of container $container_id..."
log "ERROR: Target container $container_id does not exist."
# This function accepts commands and executes them in the diagnostic container.
if ! ctr -n k8s.io tasks exec --exec-id "$exec_id" "$DIAGNOSTIC_CONTAINER_NAME" $@; then
# This function runs the AWS CLI container in containerd.
# The container is used to run the AWS CLI commands to upload files to Amazon S3.
# Clean up any existing AWS CLI container before creating a new one
log "Successfully started the AWS CLI container..."
# This function fetches stats for a give interface from the
# single values that are then dumped as JSON for each interface. Example output:
# If the interface directory does not exist, add interface with `null` value
# This function fetches an interface's statistics that indicate performance degradation using
# the `ethtool` program and dumps the stats in JSON format. Example output:
log "Degraded performance statistics for $interface not detected, OK."
# This function consolidates both `sysfs` and `ethtool` statistics into a
# JSON structure and adds them into the global JSON report object.
# For interfaces part of a VETH pair e.g. eth0@if11, we strip the name from the @ character to
# the end of the string, otherwise `ethtool` will report that the interface does not exist.
network_interfaces=$(multi_shell_exec ip -br a | grep -E '^(eth|ens|enp|eno)' | awk '{print $1}' | sed 's/@.*//')
# This function gets the range of ephemeral ports for the Linux system.
# This function gets the count of in use TCP and UDP ephemeral ports for the Linux system.
# This function gets the counts of the allocated, unused and max file descriptors of the Linux system.
# This function scans the system logs to check for a message indicating the
# system's conntrack table is full. See next comment on the different logging approaches.
conntrack_table_full_message="kernel: nf_conntrack: table full, dropping packet"
# Amazon Linux 2023 differs from traditional Linux distributions in
# its logging approach. While traditional distros write logs to /var/log/messages,
# this distribution uses `journalctl` to write system-wide logs.
log "WARN: Found conntrack table full message in system logs."
log "WARN: Found conntrack table full message in system logs."
# Check if traceroute targets were specified
# Check if DNS resolution targets were specified
DNS_RESULT=$(multi_shell_exec timeout 5s dig +short "$TARGET" 2>&1 || echo "DNS resolution failed")
log "No DNS resolution targets specified, skipping"
# This function uses tcpdump to check for TCP retransmissions in a pcap file.
# This function uses tcpdump to check for TCP Out Of Order packets in a PCAP file.
log "Checking TCP out of order packets for $interface"
tcp_out_of_order_count=$(multi_shell_exec tcpdump -r "$pcap_file_path" -n 'tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-ack != 0 and tcp[20:4] < tcp[24:4]' 2> /dev/null | wc -l)
if ! multi_shell_exec ip -br a | grep -E '^(eth|ens|enp|eno)' | grep "$interface"; then
log "WARN: Interface $interface not found, skipping packet capture."
# This function captures network packets on the given interface and checks the resulting
# PCAP files for occurrences of TCP Retransmissions, Out of Order packets etc.
# The PCAP files are uploaded to S3 for further analysis.
# For interfaces part of a VETH pair e.g. eth0@if11, we strip the name from the @ character to
# the end of the string, otherwise `tcpdump` will report that the interface does not exist.
network_interfaces=$(multi_shell_exec ip -br a | grep -E '^(eth|ens|enp|eno)' | awk '{print $1}' | sed 's/@.*//')
log "WARN: Interface $interface not found, skipping packet capture."
# Check for TCP Retransmissions and Out of Order packets and add to JSON.
# Upload the packet capture file to the S3 Bucket.
log "ERROR: Failed to upload $pcap_file_path to S3."
# Start by collecting host-level Linux statistics
# If a Container ID is specified, collect Linux statistics for it.
# Run below functions if diagnostic container is created successfully
# Render the JSON report to a file
log "Linux statistics collection completed successfully saved under $JSON_REPORT_FILE_PATH"
# Upload JSON report file to S3 bucket
log "Uploading JSON report to S3 bucket..."
log "ERROR: Failed to upload $JSON_REPORT_FILE_PATH to S3."

mainSteps.name: GenerateStatisticsOutputS3Uri
mainSteps.description: Generates the full S3 URI to the Linux statistics files to be used as the automation document's output.
